query: Kastra: Runtime authorization for Claude, Cursor, Codex and OpenClaw complaints OR frustrating OR broken

results.title: Show HN: Policy enforcement for Claude Code, Cursor, and Codex | Hacker News
results.content: | |  |  | Show HN: Policy enforcement for Claude Code, Cursor, and Codex (kastra.ai) | | 13 points by carlosjimenez1 9 days ago | hide | past | favorite | 5 comments | | Show HN: Runtime authorization for Claude Code, Cursor, and Codex Hi HN, Fernando and I built Kastra. Kastra intercepts AI agent tool calls and evaluates them against deterministic policies before they execute. This is aimed at developers using coding agents like Claude Code, Codex, Cursor, and OpenClaw.  We built Kastra after one of our Cursor agents almost executed DELETE FROM customers WHERE status='test' against a production database. We caught it before it ran, but it made us realize that nothing in our stack actually decided what the agent was allowed to do. What mattered for us wasn't the mistake; it was realizing [...] and Recon scan are free to use for developers.  If you often use Claude, Codex, Openclaw, and Cursor, Kastra can run a scan command on which risky actions your agents have already taken and automatically build rules to avoid them from happening again. Recon is a feature of Kastra that scans your local agent history. In order to run this scan, execute the commands below in your coding agent.  brew install kastra-labs/tap/kastra-edge  kastra-edge scan  The scan reads your local agent session history, and it shows all the risky actions your agent has already taken before, the secrets written to tracked files, production databases touched, force pushes, curl-to-shell, and more. This runs on your machine, and secrets never leave. In our own use cases, we kept finding things we'd forgotten or [...] so we can see what the most common patterns are and adjust policy packs for our users based on your feedback.  Documentation:   Download for MacOS Kastra Edge:   Check Kastra in action today:  | |  |     |  |  |  --- | | |  |  | josefritzishere 8 days ago  | next (javascript:void(0))   The fact that AI cannot do this on it's own is the most concerning thing of all.  reply | | | |  |  | fervinta 8 days ago  | parent | next (javascript:void(0))   Agreed. But AI is still probabilistic. We see them as a strong signal but not the final authority. We believe that authorization should stay outside the model so it is deterministic and auditable. Similar to how AWS IAM works.  reply | | | |  |  | pizzafeelsright 9 days ago  | prev | next (javascript:void(0))   after one of our Cursor agents almost

@nancy_philipExactly, we have seen our users want to customize the rules that adjust to their agentic coding style. We allow them to do so; at scale, this helps build trust, and trust allows them to delegate more work to their coding agents.

4h ago

Image 28: Ramish Zaidi

Ramish Zaidi

I've watched teams try to solve this with prompt engineering alone, telling the agent what it's allowed to touch and hoping it listens. That's not authorization, that's a suggestion. What Kastra seems to be doing is treating agent permissions the way we already treat API permissions, with enforced rules instead of good intentions. Overdue shift.

@peter_victorThat's exactly the philosophy behind Kastra. We believe authorization should sit directly in the execution path, where every tool call, shell command, API request, and file operation can be evaluated before it executes rather than remediated afterward.

results.title: they blocked OpenClaw. now what.
results.content: They have blocked OpenClaw. When I say they, Anthropic has basically announced that effective as of now, you can no longer use Claude Code with your OpenClaw subscription. And today we're going to explore what this really looks like because there's some breaking information as of right now from actual Pete Steinberger, the inventor of OpenClaw. and there is actually a workaround prompt that you can see right here that you can put in which is you would basically walk it through the models and say I want to use the provider which is Anthropic but instead of doing the normal Claude Code OAuth token that you would set up you can actually just set it up to use the actual CLI and so if you're kind of trying to follow along and this kind of sounds a little bit strange to you don't worry we're [...] to allow us to, like I'm hoping that I can just ask the Hermes Agents on my actual Spark itself to get itself configured and kind of learn about everything that's set up in there. Typically I would use, you Cursor. So I would have Cursor and I would SSH into the box and then I would run Cursor to do all the stuff because I can see all the files, I can see everything that's happening and Cursor Agent's really good at searching and everything like that. So that's kind of why I would do that. But now since I have Hermes Agent, why don't I just use something like that to get it all set up and installed? So what was the strict to make it work with Claude and OpenClaw again? So in order to do that, you'd have to basically go to here and run this command. So let's see if I can kind of copy this [...] yeah just a lot going on here. So back on the thing so if you're kind of catching up here. As of now, this is what's going on. You basically have to pay for using your OpenClaw with, you know, Anthropics services. But Pete Steinberger says like, hey, nah, you can, but as long as you have CLI, you know, Claude Code CLI, you're low-key good. And so I'm just inspecting the code and understanding what is actually happening. So it looks like the path uses the Claude binary for model inference instead of just calling the Anthropic API directly, which is what it was doing before using the OAuth token. And so OpenClaw treats it as a CLI backend provider with model references like this. So that's pretty cool. And so the way that it works is that OpenClaw launches Claude-P. So that's going to be

results.title: Working Around: Failed to extract accountId from token in OpenClaw Codex Runs
results.content:
## One Important Gotcha: Existing Sessions

Runtime selection in OpenClaw is sticky.

If your current chat session was created before applying the fix, it may still use the broken route. To avoid this, start a new session or reset the current one:

/new
or:

/reset
Then retry your request.

## Why `models status` May Still Look Wrong

After applying the workaround, running:

openclaw models status
might still report missing OpenAI authentication.

That’s expected.

This command reflects provider auth state — not the actual runtime path used during execution. Since the workaround bypasses that provider route, the status output can look misleading.

The real proof is a successful agent run showing:

agentHarnessId: codex
## What This Does Not Fix

This approach does not: [...] {

 "agents": {

 "defaults": {

 "agentRuntime": {

 "id": "codex"

 }

 }

 }

}
This explicitly routes all embedded agent runs through the Codex app-server harness instead of the PI OAuth path.

## Verifying the Fix

After restarting the gateway, confirm it’s reachable:

openclaw gateway probe
You should see a healthy response indicating the gateway is operational.

Next, run a simple test agent command:

openclaw agent --session-id workaround-smoke --message "Reply with exactly: workaround-ok" --json --timeout 90
A successful run should return:

workaround-ok
In the JSON output, verify the runtime:

{

 "agentHarnessId": "codex",

 "model": "gpt-5.5"

}
That confirms the workaround is active and working.

## One Important Gotcha: Existing Sessions [...] But:

openai/gpt-5.5

agentRuntime.id = "codex"
means:

> _Keep the model reference canonical, but execute the embedded agent turn through the native Codex runtime._

That second route avoids the token parsing logic that triggers the `Failed to extract accountId from token` error.

## The Workaround

The simplest fix is to bypass the fragile OAuth route entirely and use OpenClaw’s bundled Codex runtime plugin.

Start by setting the model to a standard OpenAI reference:

openai/gpt-5.5
Then enable the Codex plugin and force the default runtime:

openclaw config set plugins.entries.codex.enabled true --strict-json

openclaw config set agents.defaults.agentRuntime '{"id":"codex"}' --strict-json

openclaw gateway restart
The key configuration looks like this:

{

 "agents": {

 "defaults": {

Resizing the terminal still maintained the broken view. | | | |  |  | dwaltrip 29 days ago  | root | parent | prev | next (javascript:void(0))   The rendering still breaks many times a day for me, in fairly catastrophic ways. Usually because I have the audacity to resize my terminal window. Ctrl+c -> new tab -> `claude —resume` is deeply ingrained at this point. | | | |  |  | marcuschong 29 days ago  | prev | next (javascript:void(0))   It's curious that the person claiming LLMs will soon skip code entirely and go straight to binary is willing to spend $60bn on Cursor. | | | |  |  | zs234465234165 26 days ago  | parent | next (javascript:void(0))   I’m sure he has a good reason | | | |  |  | sergiotapia 29 days ago  | prev | next [...] open models and there is definitely something going on that makes some harnesses work better than others. Many of the differences are hard to pin down and some are things people don't care about. But I wouldn't say they are commodified just yet.  1. Memory use. I have colleagues complaining that Clause Code uses several GB of memory. Meanwhile I haven't heard about that regarding codex or goose, or even opencode for that matter.  2. Suitability for local models. When you use Anthropic models, you use Anthropic as a provider. They can have software between the model and your harness that will fix issues with the model. One notable thing that even the best open weights models struggle with is broken tool calls. There is a lot that a harness can do to fix broken tool calls when working with [...] VS Code (I simply use CC as a terminal in VS Code, without the plugin) as of 2 weeks ago. I think it's a combination of CC bugs + Electron(?) rendering the VS Code uses for terminal. Moved on to Zed (native Rust rendering) 2 weeks ago -> nothing flickers.  Sadly, with Fable 5 cutoff, I am actively exploring CC alternatives. Pi/OMP.sh works great as an agent (definitely better than CC). GPT is seemingly not as good as Opus, but with better agent and better skills, it probaly won't matter anyway. GPT lets you use any agent on Pro subscription. | | | |  |  | xdennis 29 days ago  | root | parent | prev | next (javascript:void(0))   Maybe flickering, but it's still broken in various ways. Only a few days ago I had an issue where the text I was typing was outside of the textbox frame. Resizing
